title: AML Agents Briefing — Sunday, 28 June 2026
script:
ALEX: Welcome to AML Agents Briefing for Sunday the 28th of June 2026. I'm Alex, joined as always by Jordan. This is your audio overview of the most material AML and financial crime developments across Asia-Pacific today. For the full obligation register, underlying sources, and horizon-scanning detail, head to amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

JORDAN: Thanks Alex. Quiet on the enforcement front today, but there are two live obligations sitting right on the doorstep for Indonesian and Australian teams, and some horizon-scanning items that deserve a proper look before the working week begins.

ALEX: Let's start with the horizon scanning, because the most consequential item this week is an AUSTRAC enforcement action that every digital asset compliance team in the region should be reading carefully. AUSTRAC imposed a civil penalty of forty-seven million Australian dollars against a registered digital currency exchange for systemic failures across three areas: transaction monitoring, mule-account detection, and suspicious matter report timeliness. This caps a twelve-month investigation, which tells us AUSTRAC did not move quickly or lightly here.

JORDAN: And the fact that all three failures were cited together is significant. This isn't a case where one control broke down. The regulator is describing a programme that failed at detection, failed at triage, and then failed to file on time even when the issues were identified. That's a governance failure as much as a technical one.

ALEX: So what's the read-across for digital currency exchanges outside Australia?

JORDAN: For compliance officers at any registered digital currency exchange, or any virtual asset service provider operating under comparable frameworks in Hong Kong, Singapore, or Malaysia, the lesson is straightforward. Pull your last six months of suspicious matter report or suspicious transaction report metrics now. Look at the gap between the date a transaction was flagged by your monitoring system and the date the report was filed. If that gap is routinely more than a few working days without a documented rationale, you have an exposure. And separately, check whether your mule-account detection logic is tuned to catch rapid post-onboarding velocity spikes. That specific pattern appears again in the Bank Negara Malaysia typology bulletin I'll cover in a moment.

ALEX: Moving on, also from AUSTRAC's horizon-scanning feed: detailed Tranche 2 industry guidance for legal practitioners has been published. This covers customer-engagement triggers, source-of-wealth diligence in conveyancing transactions, and the boundaries of legal professional privilege as it applies to suspicious matter report obligations.

JORDAN: This is one for any compliance team at a firm that provides correspondent or banking services to law practices, as much as it is for the law practices themselves. The conveyancing channel has been a known typology in Australia for years, and formalising the obligations for legal practitioners changes the risk profile of that customer segment. If you're a relationship manager or a compliance officer at an Australian bank or non-bank lender with exposure to law firm trust accounts, now is the time to revisit your customer risk assessments for that segment. The guidance has landed; your policies should reflect it.

JORDAN: Let me pick up the Bank Negara Malaysia item. BNM has published its Q1 2026 typology bulletin, focused on investment scams routed through digital banks and crypto exchanges. The mule-account indicators BNM has highlighted include a rapid velocity spike shortly after account opening, inbound transfers from multiple unrelated retail senders, and near-immediate outbound movement. None of those individually are new, but seeing them confirmed in a formal bulletin from BNM means the regulator expects you to be monitoring for them explicitly.

ALEX: And the read-across extends beyond Malaysia.

JORDAN: It does. The same pattern appears in the AUSTRAC enforcement action we just covered. If you're the MLRO or head of financial crime compliance at a digital bank or licensed exchange anywhere in the region, add BNM's three mule-account indicators to your next transaction-monitoring rule review. If your current rule set doesn't capture the combination of rapid onboarding velocity plus multi-sender inbound plus immediate outflow, that is a gap worth closing before your next supervisory interaction.

ALEX: Let's turn to obligations falling due. Two items are live right now. First, Indonesian regulated entities under the Financial Services Authority's POJK 12 framework are required to complete their annual independent review of the AML and CFT programme for the 2024 reporting period, with findings presented to both the Risk Oversight Committee and the Board of Commissioners.

JORDAN: If you're the compliance director at an Indonesian bank, multifinance company, or insurance firm, and that independent review hasn't been finalised, it needs to be your first call on Monday morning. The board-level reporting requirement is non-negotiable under the framework, and leaving findings in draft form while the deadline passes creates a governance record problem as much as a regulatory one.

ALEX: The second obligation is the HKMA annual self-assessment return under Section 11 of the HKMA AML and CFT Guideline, covering the 2024 reporting period. This is submitted via the HKMA-prescribed template and requires independent validation from Internal Audit before submission.

JORDAN: For Hong Kong AML officers, confirm with Internal Audit that their sign-off is in hand. The template itself isn't complex, but the internal validation step is the one that tends to slip when teams are running close to the deadline. Don't let a scheduling gap between compliance and audit be the reason a return goes in late.

ALEX: Before we close, the what-to-watch for your coming week. JORDAN, what are the three things listeners should have on their desk?

JORDAN: First, if you're at a digital currency exchange anywhere in the region, run a suspicious transaction report timeliness audit this week, informed by the AUSTRAC enforcement findings. Second, if your customer base includes law firms, review your customer risk assessments in light of Australia's Tranche 2 legal practitioner guidance, even if you're not an Australian-regulated entity, because other jurisdictions will follow. Third, Indonesian compliance teams should close out their POJK 12 independent review and get board-level sign-off confirmed before end of this week.

JORDAN: For the full detail behind everything we've covered, including the obligation register, source documents, and horizon-scanning summaries, visit amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

ALEX: That's the briefing for the 28th of June. Have a productive week.
